Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent this semester. I'm 29 and juggling a lot of responsibilities outside of school, so I tried to carve out time on weekends and a couple evenings during the week. I learn best by listening, so I'd often play lecture recordings while doing other tasks around the house. I aimed for about 26 hours a week of studying, but it was rarely spread evenly—some weeks I'd cram, others I'd barely touch the material.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I'd love to say I was disciplined with a fixed schedule, but the reality is that work and personal obligations often dictated when I could actually sit down to study. When assignments were due, I'd suddenly find hours I didn't know I had. Otherwise, I might go several days without opening my course materials.
Q3: Usually it was either an assignment deadline looming or needing to check if there were any announcements I'd missed. Sometimes I'd log in because I felt guilty about not being active for a while. I also checked the discussion boards occasionally to see if classmates had posted anything helpful. It wasn't really a daily habit for me—more reactive than proactive.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became more routine, usually early in the semester when I was motivated and optimistic. But honestly, that habit didn't stick for me. Life would get busy, my attendance would slip, and suddenly weeks had passed. The mindfulness exercises I started doing actually helped me feel less anxious about falling behind, which weirdly made it easier to log back in without shame.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm an auditory learner. I'd listen to them multiple times, sometimes at 1.5x speed to review concepts. The discussion boards were also useful when classmates explained things in different ways than the professor. I watched over three hours of video content because hearing explanations really helps things click for me.
Q6: I tended to skip a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. Reading on a screen strains my eyes, and I found it harder to absorb information that way compared to listening. I also didn't engage much with the optional quizzes—they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the required assignments. If something wasn't directly tied to my grade, it often got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to passing the course or understanding a difficult concept. If I could listen to it rather than read it, even better. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because those discussions sometimes clarified things better than the official materials. Basically, if it helped me feel less confused and more prepared for exams, I was in.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required work already stretched me thin with my attendance being what it was, so optional stuff got deprioritized quickly. There was always this mental calculation of 'will this actually help my grade?' and if the answer was uncertain, I'd skip it. Required activities had urgency built in; optional ones just didn't create that same pressure.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone during my commute and while cooking dinner. I also took handwritten notes that I'd review away from my computer. A lot of my actual learning happened with headphones in, away from the LMS entirely. I'd estimate at least half my study time was invisible to the system because I wasn't actively logged in.
Q10: So many times! I'd download a week's worth of audio lectures and spend hours listening and re-listening while doing chores or exercising. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also had study sessions with a couple of classmates where we'd discuss concepts over coffee—real learning was happening, but the LMS had no idea. My peer interactions were actually higher than my login frequency might suggest.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video lecture once to download it, then watch it two or three more times offline. Same with any PDFs I actually did use—I'd save them to my tablet and review them later. The LMS probably showed me accessing something once when in reality I engaged with it multiple times. This was my main strategy for managing my time and studying when it was convenient for me.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when stress peaked and my resilience felt depleted. I'd look at the workload and feel overwhelmed, which made me avoid logging in altogether. Family obligations also intensified around that time, and I found myself choosing between attending to those and keeping up with coursework. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me push through those moments without spiraling.
Q13: All of those factors affected me at different points. Fatigue was huge—being 29 and managing multiple responsibilities meant I was often exhausted by evening when I'd planned to study. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely for a while. When I was overwhelmed, the LMS became something I dreaded opening because it just reminded me of everything I was behind on.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd log in, click through some materials, maybe start a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I was going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out due to stress or exhaustion. Those sessions probably showed as 'activity' but they weren't productive learning at all.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important. Sometimes the anxiety of starting something difficult was worse than the guilt of putting it off. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was fresher, but tomorrow would bring its own challenges. My moderate anxiety made starting tasks feel like climbing a mountain, even when logically I knew they were manageable.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I'd drift and lose focus on the course entirely. As a deadline approached, my LMS activity would spike dramatically—I'd suddenly be watching videos, checking discussion posts, and downloading materials all at once. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but that pressure was what got me to complete things. My 45% assignment completion rate reflects how I sometimes miscalculated how much time I had.
Q17: When I received feedback, especially positive or constructive feedback, it motivated me to engage more. Knowing someone was actually looking at my work made it feel more worthwhile. However, if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and disengage. The times I did well on assignments, I felt encouraged to put more effort into the next one. Feedback was like fuel for my motivation.
Q18: The course structure was a mixed bag for me. Having recorded lectures was a lifesaver because of my auditory learning style and irregular schedule. But the pacing sometimes felt relentless—new content kept coming whether I was ready or not. I appreciated having some flexibility, but I also could have used more built-in checkpoints to keep me accountable. The structure worked for students with more regular schedules than mine.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was when important information was only in text format or when deadlines clustered together. I also found the discussion boards a bit clunky to navigate, which discouraged me from participating more. Clearer organization and more audio-based resources would have helped someone like me stay engaged more consistently.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are anxious or overwhelmed—they're logging in, clicking around, maybe rewatching videos trying to understand, but they're stuck and can't translate that activity into completed work. It might also be perfectionism, where someone keeps reviewing but never feels ready to submit. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity; sometimes it's just spinning wheels.
Q21: That actually resonates with me quite a bit. My LMS activity was relatively low, but I still passed with a decent exam score because so much of my learning happened offline. I'd listen to downloaded lectures repeatedly, study from my own notes, and discuss with peers outside the system. Someone looking at my login frequency might think I wasn't trying, but I was just learning in ways the platform couldn't track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. That's useful for identifying students who might be disengaging entirely. But logs miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and the mental and emotional state of the learner. They can't tell if I'm genuinely understanding something or just letting a video play while I zone out. Context is everything they can't capture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions to pass. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, engagement feels like being mentally present and invested, even when it's challenging. It's not about how many times I log in—it's about whether the learning is sticking and whether I'm growing from it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how the mindfulness intervention genuinely helped my mental state and allowed me to re-engage when I was struggling. It can't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures while walking or the conversations with classmates that clarified difficult concepts. It also can't show my determination to pass despite everything working against me this semester—my resilience might be low by some measures, but I'm still here, still climbing.
